coredata.dc:title: A novel method for measuring cake porosity by ions detection technique in a conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration process
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Yun
Wang, Xujia
Wang, Zhenqiong
Yan, Fanyong
Zu, FanLin
Zhang, Hongwei
coredata.dc:description: Cake porosity plays an important role for evaluating membrane fouling in low-pressure membrane filtration process. Current method for calculating cake porosity is based on Carman-Kozeny equation . It would have many limitations due to the indirect analysis. CLSM has been applied to directly measure the structure of chemical particle models, but requirement that materials need to be fluorescent is difficult to achieve in conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration processes. Our study investigated a novel method using fluorescence probe to measure cake porosity for making up the defect of non-fluorescent of cake layer in conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration process. Two metal ions probes, quercetin and carbon nanodots (CNDs), were applied to detect the Al3+ ions and Fe3+ ions from Al-based salt and Fe-based salt coagulants respectively. Cake morphology could be directly observed by an established method (CLSM and image analysis technique). Using t-test to compare the obtained porosities with those calculated from specific cake resistance by Carman–Kozeny equation. Result shows that they are not significantly different at the confidence level of 95% and 98%, which proves fluorescence identification has higher accuracy. Furthermore, characters of the complexing formed by different cations and hydroxide ions also affect the cake porosity. Porosities of Al-based cake are smaller and those of Fe-based cake are larger. Effect of both fractal dimension and floc size on porosity were also investigated and concluded that role of the fractal dimension is prior to floc size.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cake porosity
Coagulation-ultrafiltration
Metal ion probe
CLSM
Image analysis
Cake property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism between hydrolyzed metal ions and fluorescent dyes
Measurement of cake porosity using a CLSM and image analysis
Calculation of cake porosity from the measured specific cake resistance and Carman–Kozeny equation
Fluorescent staining and observation by CLSM
Characters of aggregates in different conditions
Comparison of porosities from two methods
Dependence of cake porosity on the cationic species of coagulants and cake properties
Dependence of cake porosity on the diameter and fractal dimension of aggregates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism of producing fluorescence: (a) chemical formula of quercetin combined with Al3+. (b) left: emission peak of CNDs; right: absorption peak of curcumin.
Contrast of original floc image and fluorescent images (Al-based floc stained by QCT). (a) Original floc image. (b) Fluorescent image from FITC tunnel. (c) Fluorescent image from TRITC tunnel.
Schematic diagram of coagulation-ultrafiltration setup in the study.
CLSM images and binary images for Run A: (a) Al-based cake slices stained by quercetin. (b) Fe-based cake slices stained by CNDs. The size of each image is 635.283 μm × 635.283 μm.
Comparison of cake porosity between the conditions of adding Al-based coagulant and Fe-based coagulant.
Change trend of porosity and floc size with different fractal dimensions. Columnar pattern represents porosity and polyline represents floc size.
Different aggregate conditions and corresponding properties.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: da : floc diameter; Df : fractal dimension, more details are in Supplementary Material.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Partial parameters used in calculating cake porosity.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Rc: cake resistance; αc: specific cake resistance. The values are determined by average value of 3 measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of porosities from experiment and CLSM analysis.

head.ce:title: A novel method for measuring cake porosity by ions detection technique in a conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration process

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Process & School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Process & School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cake porosity plays an important role for evaluating membrane fouling in low-pressure membrane filtration process. Current method for calculating cake porosity is based on Carman-Kozeny equation . It would have many limitations due to the indirect analysis. CLSM has been applied to directly measure the structure of chemical particle models, but requirement that materials need to be fluorescent is difficult to achieve in conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration processes. Our study investigated a novel method using fluorescence probe to measure cake porosity for making up the defect of non-fluorescent of cake layer in conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration process. Two metal ions probes, quercetin and carbon nanodots (CNDs), were applied to detect the Al3+ ions and Fe3+ ions from Al-based salt and Fe-based salt coagulants respectively. Cake morphology could be directly observed by an established method (CLSM and image analysis technique). Using t-test to compare the obtained porosities with those calculated from specific cake resistance by Carman–Kozeny equation. Result shows that they are not significantly different at the confidence level of 95% and 98%, which proves fluorescence identification has higher accuracy. Furthermore, characters of the complexing formed by different cations and hydroxide ions also affect the cake porosity. Porosities of Al-based cake are smaller and those of Fe-based cake are larger. Effect of both fractal dimension and floc size on porosity were also investigated and concluded that role of the fractal dimension is prior to floc size.

ce:section.ce:para: In the membrane filtration process, characteristic of cake layer is a valuable indicator of membrane fouling [1,2]. Cake layer are formed by particles deposited on the membrane surface. Formation of cake layer would increase hydraulic resistance, reduce permeate flux and filtration efficiency [3,4]. Therefore, research on cake morphology has gained wide attention in these years. Cake resistance strongly depends on several factors such as aggregate size, aggregate shape and hydraulic condition, etc [5]. Among these physical properties parameters, cake porosity are representative due to its significance for reflecting the cake structure and compressibility [6,7].

Currently, measuring porosity based on filtration principles is the most widely used method in the water treatment area [8–10]. It requires a constant pressure condition and calculates porosity by measuring the permeate flux. Nevertheless, this method has a limitation that they are indirect analysis of cake porosity [6]. Therefore, more research began to explore methods of direct measurement using image analysis. In this process, technique of confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) is outperformed for allowing completing parameters measurement and internal structure observation without destroying the sample [11–14]. This method is not only applied in the biological field, but also used to analyze cake layer composed of chemical field. Park et al. [6] combined CLSM technique and image analysis to measure cake porosity formed by fluorescent polystyrene latex beads. They found the results are almost identical to those measured by traditional Carman–Kozeny equation, which proved that CLSM can be used to directly analyze cake porosity formed from chemical flocs. Hassan et al. [15] developed an in suit method to characterize cake structure formed by depth dyed fluorescent microspheres using CLSM, and investigated the particle arrangement, homogeneity and porosity in dead end microfiltration experiment. Wu et al. [16] used CLSM to locate engineered nanomaterials (fluorescent CdTe quantum dots) in different depths of fouled ultrafiltration membranes. However, material selected in these researches are self-fluorescent. This requirement is impossible to accomplish in a conventional coagulation-microfiltration process because non-fluorescent suspended solids were used as primary particles [17]. Therefore, metal ions probe was used to complex the metal ions dispersed in cake layer for exciting fluorescence signal. Considering that the most commonly used inorganic salt coagulants are mainly Al-base or Fe-base, our study selected quercetin and carbon nanodots (CNDs) as dyes. These two substances could be combined with Al3+ ions and Fe3+ ions respectively to form fluorescent complexing. Fluorescence could be captured by the CLSM to generate images.

In this paper, we report the feasibility of using ions detection technique to measure cake porosity in conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration process. To validate this conjecture, an established method was used to construct the experimental system. Porosity measured by CLSM were statistically compared with those calculated from Carman–Kozeny equation under different conditions. Based on this novel method for measuring cake porosity, we investigated the reasons on different porosities with different cationic coagulants and effect of physical properties of the particle such as floc size and fractal dimensions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism between hydrolyzed metal ions and fluorescent dyes
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probe technique is of great interest for its flexible operation, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity in recent years [18]. our study use quercetin and carbon nanodots (CNDs) to detect metal ions from coagulants. Quercetin (3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone, QCT) is the most common flavonoids presented in nature. It has the advantages of attractive anticancer, antimutagenic and good curative effect for cardiovascular disease [19–21]. Researches show that, QCT and Al3+ could form into stable complexing(Fig. 1 a) [21,22]. Fluorescence properties are realized by the inhibition of excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) in the complexing process [23]. Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are nanometer-grade particles that smaller than 10 nm [24]. Its appearance has made up much defects of traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [25,26]. The coordination interaction between phenolic hydroxy groups of CNDs and Fe3+ ions could quench the fluorescence of CNDs. This mechanism was usually applied for detecting Fe3+ ions or other colored reactions [27]. But our study would use a new enhanced fluorescence probe to obtain a better observation effect. This new probe was produced by CNDs and curcumin basing on internal filter effect [28]. As Fig. 1b, the emission peak of CNDs and the absorption peak of curcumin are at almost coincided position, peaking at 420 nm. The internal filter generated from mixture would quench the fluorescence of CNDs. After adding Fe3+ ions, curcumin would react with them to cause a decrease of absorption peak wavelength and the fluorescence would be quenched, accordingly the fluorescence of CNDs could be released. The two dyes come from previous study in our group.

Meanwhile, contrast of original floc image and fluorescent images are shown in Fig. 2 . Fluorescent images are shot byfluorescence microscope. Image in Fig. 2(b) and (c) are from different fluorescence tunnels. It can be seen from the figures that all fluorescent images are very similar to the original image, which indicating that fluorescent ion probe can describe the graphic outline.

We will use graphical analysis to further prove the consistency of images. According to the definition of two-dimensional fractal dimension, fractal dimension (D2) a power law relation between projected area (As) and the characteristic length (l) of the aggregates, namely [29]:
(1)
As ∼
l
D 2

Two-dimensional fractal dimension (D2) is the slope obtained from linear fits to the power-law regions of the data. But because Fig. 2 has only one set of pictures, it is difficult to calculate fractal dimension according to this method. Therefore, our study will directly compare the ratio of Log projected area (As) and Log characteristic length (l) of the three images. Values are measured in pixels, which is shown in Table S1. Ratios of the graph (a), graph (b) and graph (c) are 1.79, 1.80 and 1.81 respectively, which proves that the fluorescence images are basically consistent with the original image.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of cake porosity using a CLSM and image analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Cake porosities would be determined by following procedures. (a) Using the Z axis scanning program of CLSM to capture optical sectioned images. (b) All sectioned images were converted to gray-scale images whose intensity ranging of 0–255, then converted to binary images by Otsu [30] threshold segmentation. (c) Counting black and white pixels points for calculating the porosity of per binary images. Black parts correspond to vacancy. Considering that the cake layer has a certain thickness, the following integral equation was applied to obtain an accurate value [6]:
(2)
ε
CLSM
=
A
∫ 0 T
ε z
d z
AT
=
∫ 0 T
ε z
d z
T
where (A) is the area of view field of image; (εz ) the sectional porosity at a distance z from first page. Set interval is 2 μm. (T) is the cake thickness that depends on sheet numbers. Porosities measured by the above methods were compared with those calculated by the experimental model [31]. Statistical methods were used to examine the fitting degree between them for verifying the feasibility of program. Calculating course will be described in more detail in Section 4.2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of cake porosity from the measured specific cake resistance and Carman–Kozeny equation
ce:section.ce:para:
Measured porosities would be derived by Darcy’s law and Carman–Kozeny equation. The total membrane resistance (Rt ) was partitioned into membrane intrinsic resistance (Rm ), adsorption resistance of membrane fouling (Rf ) and cake resistance (Rc ), namely:
(3)
R t =
R m
+
R f
+
R c

From Darcy's law, relationship between the three resistance and membrane flux (J) is as follows:
(4)
J =
Δ P
μ
R t
=
Δ P
μ (
R m
+
R f
+
R c
)
where J is the flux of membrane filtration, m/s; △P is the trans-membrane pressure of each sample, Pa; μ is the viscosity of water at 20◦C, Pa·s. In this paper, negative pressure suction was used as driving force to simulate the operation mode in industry. Therefore, trans-membrane pressure is not constant and it needs to be measured separately for each specimen. Values of Rm , Rf and Rc would be calculated by:
(5)
R m
=
Δ
P m
μ
J m
R f
=
Δ
P f
μ
J f
-
R m
R c
=
Δ P
μ J
-
R m
-
R f
where ΔPm and ΔPf are trans-membrane pressure at different stages, Jm and Jf are corresponded to them. The cake resistance Rc is related to the cake load (m′) and the specific cake resistance (αc ), thus αc is obtained by:
(6)
α c
=
R c
m ′
=
R c
A m
M
=
R c
A m
C a
V
where αc is the specific cake resistance, m/mg; C a is the aggregate concentration in coagulated mixture, mg/L; V is the volume of the filtrate, L. In a resistance-in-series model, cake mass is difficult to weight. As the coagulated suspension maintained its homogeneous status throughout the filtration process, cake mass could be obtained from the product of filtrate volume and aggregate concentration. A m is the effective membrane surface area, which was 0.00785 m2. Since the size of the aggregates were relatively large, the effects of concentration polarization could be negligible [6].

Meanwhile, according to the well-known Carman-Kozeny equation, αc can also be determined by particle size (dp ) and cake porosity (εc ),
(7)
α c
=
36
K
′ ′
( 1 -
ε c
)
ρ c
d p 2
ε c 3
where K″ is the empirical Kozeny constant, which is assumed to be 5 in this study [32], ρ p is the cake density and d p is a diameter of internal particles of the compressed cake. Synthesizing the above equations to obtain cake porosities (εc ). Calculation methods of various parameters will be described in detail in Section 3.2.

In our study, water sample is the lake water. It belongs to natural water and contains a lot of natural organic matter (NOM). Specific data of water quality is summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that water turbidity is low, but the content of the organic suspended solid is relatively high. Character of water sample is generally acidic. All characters shows a state of micro-polluted water.

An experimental setup was built-up for forming a cake layer as Fig. 3 . Coagulation process and filtration process are carried out simultaneously in a cylindrical vessel. Selecting the colloidal suspensions in natural water as primary particles, AlCl3·6H2O(AR, Tianjin, China) and FeCl3·6H2O(AR, Tianjin, China) as coagulants.. In order to facilitate the observation, it is necessary to ensure that cake layer has a certain thickness. According to the principle of coagulation, more flocs can be formed and floc structure is most stable at this time when sweep function is dominant. For maintaining sweep status, dosage of metal ions needs to be more than the optimum value. We determine the boundary of sweep function by the change of turbidity and zeta potential when adding different coagulants. As in Fig. S1, steady dose of Al3+ and Fe3+ are both 80 mg/L. Therefore, we ensure the concentration of both metal ions to be 100 mg/L. To obtain diverse floc characteristics, mixing intensity would be controlled as a variable, ranging of 100–400 r/s by an electric agitator (MICROSTAR 7.5 control, IKA-Werke GmbH & CO., Germany). Mixing requires to be sustained until the end of filtration. A PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) ultrafiltration membrane (UF100, Rising Sun Membrane Technology Co., Ltd., China) was employed as filter and was immersed in deionized water for 24 h for removing the residual additives before using [13]. The pressure provided by flow-fixed peristaltic pump (BT101S-CE, Lead Fluid Technology Co., Ltd., China) is measured by pressure sensor (MIK-P300, Asmik, China) then transferred into a paperless recorder (MIK-RX9600, Asmik, China). Filtrate mass is weighted by an electronic balance (UX6200H, Shimadzu, Japan) in order to calculate the permeate flux.

For calculating cake porosities, all required parameters are obtained by the following steps. Since the pressure of filtration system is not constant, data of trans-membrane pressure and water flux at each stage were recorded independently. Deionized water is used as stock solution to determine the specific cake resistance. Using the new membrane as filter to obtain ΔPm and Jm . Using the filter cake layer attached to the membrane as filter to obtain ΔP and J. Using the polluted membrane without cake layer as filter to obtain ΔPf and Jf . Aggregate concentration Ca was calculated by the method for measuring the suspended solids(SS). Cake layer is soft and fragile, thence conventional methods are not suitable for measuring its density. Since the shape of cake is approximately a cylinder, cake thickness would be measured by a dial thickness gauges (TESA, Switzerland) to calculate the density ρc . Samples should be measured in a wet condition to guarantee the accuracy of results. Using a Nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer (Mastersizer 3000, Malvern, UK) to measure the size of suspended particles. The suspended particles are from the supernatant of waste liquid produced by flushing cake layer. Then we can obtain values of particle diameter dp .

Floc characteristics are mainly reflected by floc size and fractal dimension. Both parameters could be measured by laser particle sizer (Mastersizer 2000, Malvern, UK). D(0.5) is the value that has the highest frequency of particle size distribution, and regarded as the average diameter of aggregate. Cake layer would be compressed by external pressure during the filtration process, therefore aggregate diameter mentioned here cannot be confused with the particle size of cake. Fractal dimension (
D f
) is widely used in describing complexity and chaos of aggregates. To measure this parameter, small-angle laser light scattering (SALLS) was employed in this study. There are 52 multi-angle photosensitive detectors to receive the laser light beam scattered by samples [33]. Fractal dimension (
D f
) was taken as the slope of log-log scale of data obtained from linear fits, which is given by [34]:
(8)
I
( q )
∞
q
- D f
where I is the total scattered light intensity that could be exported from apparatus, and Q is scattering vector, which is calculated by:
(9)
Q =
4 π n sin (
θ
/
2 )
λ

n is the refractive index of dispersant; θ is scattered angle obtained from instrument manufacturer; λ the wavelength of laser in vacuum, 633 nm in our study. Commonly, higher values of Df mean more compact internal structure of aggregate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent staining and observation by CLSM
ce:section.ce:para: Cake layer consists of colloidal particles in natural waters. It is highly susceptible to cracking. Therefore samples needs to be embedded before observation. After completing filtration process, cutting cake layer into small blocks about 1 cm * 1cm to be stained with fluorescent probe. Humid samples should be rapidly sealed in the glass bottom dishes with 1.5% agar solution and stored at 4 °C. This procedure was used to avoid cracks on cake layer due to water loss. Afterwards, CLSM (FV1200, Olympus, Japan) was used to observe the structure of samples. Fluorescence of quercetin and CNDs were detected with a laser source at 488 nm and 405 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characters of aggregates in different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain different cake property, we provided different hydraulic conditions to form different floc structure. Floc characters (diameter, da, and fractal dimension, Df) would be produced by changing coagulant species and mixing intensity. As Table 2 , results indicated that aggregate size would increase first then decrease. It got the maximum size at speed of 200r/s (G = 100 s−1). Because high shear rate would apply to the weakest points of aggregate and break them into fragmentations smaller than the parent floc [35]. Both Al-based coagulant and Fe-based coagulant can reflect this phenomenon. In this study, dosage of coagulants is large, which causes a larger number of flocs. In Table 3 , ζ potential is positive and much greater than 0, thus sweep function is dominant. Al3+ and Fe3+ are hydrolyzed to generate precipitation. During the sedimentation process, these sediments can sweep and hunt the colloidal particles in the water and make them adhered [36].

Studies have shown that iron hydroxide complexes have better adhesion properties (i.e, stronger interaction between particles) than aluminum hydroxide complexes [37]. This principle caused Fe-based aggregate diameter is generally larger than Al-based aggregate diameter. But the perform of fractal dimensions is reversed. Considering the correlation between the parameters and floc structure, it can be thought that Al-based aggregates are more compact and Fe-based aggregates have looser texture. Structure of cake layers formed by the two aggregates is also affected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of porosities from two methods
ce:section.ce:para: Cake porosities was calculated separately by two methods, e.g., (i) CLSM and image analysis, (ii) Carman-Kozeny equation. These samples would exhibit different fluorescence colors by different metal ions probes, where Al-based cake dyed with quercetin emits yellow light and Fe-based cake dyed with CNDs emits blue light. According to Section 2.2, captured images are processed by software to calculate porosities. Partial sliced images and binary images will be shown in Fig. 4 . Principle of 3D-image in CLSM is that emitted laser is focused on a focal plane, which is set as datum plane for repeating the scanning of multiple planes to complete the observation in true means of depth-wise of specimen [38]. Therefore, the picture would show a change in brightness.

Specific cake resistance was determined by Carman-Kozeny equation according to Section 2.3. Due to the driving force provided by peristaltic pump is not constant, data of pressure would fluctuate within a certain amplitude. In order to obtain concrete pressure values, these scattered points were linearly fitted. It proves that the value is credible (R2 > 0.99). Calculating the parameters of each stage by bringing them into Eqs. (5) and (6). The data will be shown in Table 4 .

Porosities calculated by the above methods are shown in Table 5 . From the results, it can be intuitively seen that values of calculated porosity are very close to those of measured porosity. In order to eliminate the effect of random errors, our study use to perform t-test to perform a statistically examine. The t values of the two samples would be calculated separately, according to the following equation:
(10)
t =
x
¯
s
n
( s =
∑
i = 0
n
( x i -
x
¯
)
2
n - 1
)
where xi is the difference among the porosities(ε),
x ¯
is the average of xi , s is the standard deviation of samples, n is sample number (n = 4), n-1 is the degree of freedom. Calculated values are 2.989 (Al-based group) and 4.399 (Fe-based group), then they are not significantly different at the confidence level of 95% and 98% respectively at the freedom degree of 3 [39]. It is resulted that at least under the conditions of using Al-based or Fe-based coagulants, ions detection technique is suitable for measuring cake porosity composed of natural organic matter (NOM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of cake porosity on the cationic species of coagulants and cake properties
ce:section.ce:para: Coagulants with different cations would give different porosities. The contrast between Al-based coagulant and Fe-based coagulant are presented in Fig. 5 . Porosity of cake formed by Fe-based coagulant is integrally large, ranging of 65–70%; but porosity formed by Al-based coagulant are smaller, ranging of 37–42%. Qiao et al. [40] found that FeCl3 can effectively slow down the decline of permeate flux, which is in good agreement with our porosity results. Their significant difference illustrates that cation type of coagulant is also an important affecting factor for porosity. As shown in Fig. 4, Al-based cake is denser than Fe-based cake. Because the initial particle size of Al3+ hydrolysis is mostly 2–2.5 μm, and that of Fe3+ hydrolysis is 10–30 μm [41]. Structure of the aggregate formed by the former is more compact. But as Table 6 , particle size of Al-based cake is intuitively larger than that of Fe-based cake. This phenomenon arises from the combination of coagulation mechanism, compression process and coagulant hydrolysis. Under sweep function, flocs are adhered by a plurality of micelles. And micelles are composed of core, adsorption layer and diffusion layer [42]. The formation of the cake layer is accompanied by the non-spontaneous accumulation of flocs. This process can be considered that a more loosely diffusion layer is compressed and coated on the colloidal particle composed of the colloidal core and adsorption layer. The pH of the raw water is about 6.0, thus the dominant cores after adding coagulants are Al(OH)2+ and Fe(OH)2+ separately. At the same complex level, the thixotropy of the Fe3+ hydration product exceeds that of the Al3+ hydration product. Fe-based flocs would break into relatively large-sized fragments by high shear force [41,43]. However, the monomer hydrolyzate(Ala) existed on the surface of AlCl3 precipitate makes the mutual cluster of micro-flocs [44], which causes the Al-based fragments to re-grow with a size larger than Fe-based particles during the deposition process. The phenomenon that Al-based cake thickness is greater than Fe-based cake thickness may also be related to the difference of particle size. It can be seen from Table 6 that the densities are close, whether the density is affected by particle size is difficult to assess because it is not only determined by volume but also by cake mass. Since the particle numbers and the mass of each particle are almost unmeasurable in the cake, the parameter, density, are discussed only as a reference here. From Eq. (7), porosity is derived from the combination of density and particle size. Under the premise that densities of the two cakes are basically consistent, the one with a smaller particle size would have a larger porosity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of cake porosity on the diameter and fractal dimension of aggregates
ce:section.ce:para:
Park et al. [6] found that effect of the fractal dimension on the cake porosity was negligible for which is less than 2.4, but for that above 2.4 is negatively correlated. In Table 2, except for the value of Run Fe-A, all other fractal dimensions are more than 2.4. The discussion of data trend can follow this phenomenon in this study. Because our study uses tiny particles in water as raw substances, difference of aggregate size is relatively large with changing single variable. It also needs to be considered the influence of floc size when discussing the affecting factors on porosity. In order to avoid the impact of observation errors, we will use values calculated from specific cake resistance for analysis. Fig. 6 represents the porosities as a function of floc diameter with different coagulants. They demonstrate the dependence of cake porosity on the aggregate diameter and fractal dimension. As shown in Fig. 6(a), values of four fractal dimensions are consistent, but the difference of porosity is also obvious. Taking Run Al-C and Run Al-D as examples, they have the same fractal dimensions but different floc size(dAl-C > dAl-D). This phenomenon causes that porosity of Al-C is more than that of Al-D( ε
Al-C> ε
Al-D). It indicates that porosity would increase following the growth of floc size without considering the fractal dimension. The reason we considered is that larger aggregates are looser under the same fractal conditions, which is further affecting cake composition. Therefore, change on porosity of each group in figure is consistent with those of floc size.

Certainly, porosity is still largely dependent on the fractal dimension. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the fractal dimension of Run Fe-A(Df-Fe-A) is 2.09, which is much smaller than the other fractal values in the same group. Although its aggregate size is not the largest, the porosity is the largest yet. According to this phenomenon, we think that the negative correlation between porosity and fractal dimension is not only suitable for the case where fractal dimension is more than 2.4. Actually, the fractal dimensions of the other three groups in Fig. 6(b) are very close and corresponding difference on porosity is also small. Therein, the porosity of Run Fe-B is the smallest even if the aggregate diameter is relatively large due to having the largest fractal dimension. Therefore, it is suspected that the effect of fractal dimension on porosity is prior to that of aggregate size. In the case that aggregate diameters of Run Fe-C and Run Fe-D are close, former's fractal dimension is more than the latter's and their porosity conditions are also ε
Fe-C> ε
Fe-D. They are negatively correlated and consistent with existing conclusions. Therefore, judging the change of porosity requires to synthesize various influencing factors.

This study verified the feasibility of using ions detection technique to measure cake porosity in conventional coagulation-ultrafiltration. By statistically comparing measuring porosity(εCLSM) and calculating porosity(εc), our study determined the effectiveness of this method. Concrete conclusions are as follows:
1.
The two results are not significantly different at the confidence level of 95% and 98%, which demonstrates that metal ion probes are suitable for measuring cake porosity.
2.
Different cations of coagulants would result in different porosity ranges. Porosities of Al-based cake are smaller and those of Fe-based cake are larger. Characters of agglomerates complexing different cations and hydroxide ions affects the floc properties, then determines the difference in porosity.
3.
Cake porosities are affected by both aggregate size and the fractal dimension.
However, the role of the fractal dimension is prior to aggregate size.

The above results prove that, ions detection technique is suitable for identifying the free cations from coagulants, then further measuring the cake porosity. This method provides a theoretical basis for on-line measuring cake porosity in conventional coagulation-membrane filtration system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [No. 51638011] and [No. 51878448], National Program on Key Research Project [No. YS2017YFGH000386], Tianjin Science and Technology Plan Project [No. 16PTGCCX00070].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2019.03.061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
